industry.headline: SECI Issues Transformer Tender for 700 MW Solar Project
industry.summary: The Solar Energy Corporation of India (SECI) has invited bids for engineering, design, manufacturing, testing, and supply of five 160 MVA, 220/33-33 KV power transformers for a 700 MW solar project at Radhanesda in Gujarat. The tender includes specifications for technical parameters and compliance. Successful bidders will deliver transformers per agreed terms. This procurement aims to expand solar infrastructure in India.

tech.headline: Chinese researchers achieve 33.33% efficiency in tandem solar cells
tech.summary: CAS-led researchers developed a peak-selective passivation strategy using aluminum oxide nanocoating to address leakage in pyramid-textured silicon for perovskite-silicon tandem cells. The method is compatible with existing silicon manufacturing and enables high-efficiency, stable devices reaching around 33% efficiency.

learn.title: Bifacial Solar Panels Power Both Sides
learn.intro: Bifacial modules capture sunlight on their front and rear, boosting yield without extra land. In crowded rooftops and large‑scale farms, this technology can lift output 10‑30% and accelerate the clean‑energy shift.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a mirror‑backed solar panel that works like a two‑sided flashlight. Light hitting the front creates electricity, while reflected ground albedo hits the back, adding up to 15 % more power per module.
learn.c2_text: Both faces share the same photovoltaic cells connected in series. A transparent back sheet lets diffuse light reach the rear cells, while bypass diodes protect against shading, keeping efficiency high even on cloudy days.
learn.c3_text: India’s solar parks in Rajasthan use sand‑reflective ground cover, raising bifacial gains to 20 %. The Ministry’s 2023 incentive scheme rewards farms that achieve >15 % bifacial gain, accelerating capacity growth.
learn.stat1: Global bifacial capacity reached 45 GW in 2023
learn.stat2: Bifacial modules can deliver 10‑30 % higher energy yield
learn.stat3: India installed 12 GW bifacial PV in 2024
learn.stat4: Albedo‑enhanced farms can reach 1 500 kWh/kW‑p annually

learn.title: India ISTS Waiver Cuts Renewable Costs
learn.intro: The Inter‑State Transmission System (ISTS) waiver removes costly transmission charges for renewable power, unlocking cheaper clean energy across state borders and spurring investment in India’s green grid.
learn.c1_text: Think of a toll‑free highway for electricity. By waiving ISTS fees, generators save up to ₹0.30 kWh⁻¹, making solar and wind projects financially tighter and more attractive to investors.
learn.c2_text: Without transmission levies, power can flow from high‑resource states like Gujarat to demand centers such as Delhi at lower tariffs, improving capacity utilisation and reducing curtailment by 5‑7 %.
learn.c3_text: Since the 2022 waiver, India’s renewable‑only inter‑state trades grew 18 % YoY, cutting average renewable procurement costs from ₹4.5/kWh to ₹4.0/kWh in 2024.
learn.stat1: India’s renewable capacity reached 190 GW in 2024
learn.stat3: Inter‑state renewable trade rose to 45 TWh in 2024

learn.title: Offshore Wind: Design to Grid
learn.intro: Offshore wind farms harness stronger sea breezes, delivering large, low‑cost power blocks. Understanding turbine layout, foundation types, and grid links is key to scaling this clean resource worldwide.
learn.c1_text: Picture a floating chessboard: turbines are spaced 7‑10 rotor diameters apart to avoid wake loss, allowing each 12‑MW turbine to generate up to 50 GWh annually.
learn.c2_text: Monopiles, jackets, or floating platforms anchor turbines. Installation uses specialized jack‑up vessels; floating farms can be deployed in water >60 m deep, expanding site options dramatically.
learn.c3_text: The 1.2‑GW Gujarat offshore project uses jacket foundations and a 220‑kV subsea cable, expected to cut regional emissions by 5 Mt CO₂e per year, showcasing India’s emerging offshore sector.
learn.stat1: Global offshore capacity hit 55 GW in 2023
learn.stat2: Capacity factor averages 50 % offshore vs 25 % on‑shore
learn.stat3: Cost per MWh fell to $1,800 in 2024

learn.title: Colour Codes: Blue, Green & Grey Hydrogen
learn.intro: Hydrogen’s colour labels reveal how it’s made and its carbon footprint. Knowing the differences helps policymakers and investors steer funds toward truly low‑carbon fuels.
learn.c1_text: Like burning natural gas in a kitchen, grey hydrogen is produced via steam‑methane reforming (SMR) without carbon capture, emitting about 10 kg CO₂ per kg H₂ produced.
learn.c2_text: Green hydrogen comes from electrolysis powered by renewable electricity. With zero direct emissions, its cost hinges on renewable power price—currently $3–$5 per kg in regions with surplus solar.
learn.c3_text: India’s 2024 pilot blends SMR with carbon capture, producing 0.5 Mt of blue H₂ and cutting CO₂ by 80 % compared with grey. The scheme aims for 2 Mt blue H₂ by 2030.
learn.stat1: World hydrogen demand projected at 140 Mt by 2030
learn.stat2: Green H₂ production capacity reached 30 GW in 2024
learn.stat3: India installed 12 GW of electrolyzers in 2024

learn.title: Carbon Markets Power Clean Energy
learn.intro: Carbon markets assign a price to emissions, rewarding projects that cut CO₂. Credits generated by renewable or efficiency schemes can fund new clean‑energy build‑outs, especially in emerging economies.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a farmer earning vouchers for each tonne of CO₂ avoided. Those vouchers—carbon credits—can be sold to companies needing to meet compliance or voluntary targets, creating a revenue stream for projects.
learn.c2_text: Credits are verified, registered, and retired to avoid double counting. Prices vary: EU ETS traded around €80/tCO₂ in 2024, while voluntary markets averaged $12/tCO₂, influencing project economics differently.
learn.c3_text: India’s CDM and recent REC‑linked carbon credit scheme funded 5 GW of solar in 2023, delivering $200 million in extra capital and helping meet the nation’s 2030 emissions goal.
learn.stat1: Global carbon market volume reached 7 Gt CO₂e in 2023
learn.stat3: Renewable projects earned 1.2 MtCO₂e credits in India 2023
learn.stat4: Clean‑energy financing via credits grew 23 % YoY

learn.title: Climate Finance & COP Deals for Developing Nations
learn.intro: COP negotiations shape billions of dollars of climate finance, crucial for developing countries to build resilient energy systems. Understanding the pledges and delivery mechanisms reveals pathways to a fair energy transition.
learn.c1_text: Think of a global savings pool: at COP28, developed nations pledged $115 billion for the 2025‑2030 period, earmarked for renewable, adaptation, and loss‑and‑damage projects in vulnerable economies.
learn.c2_text: Funds flow through multilateral development banks, green climate funds, and blended finance. Guarantees lower risk, allowing private investors to co‑fund solar, wind, and storage projects at lower cost of capital.
learn.c3_text: India accessed $10 billion from the Green Climate Fund for 5 GW of offshore wind and 3 GW of storage, accelerating its 2030 renewable target and creating 150 k jobs in coastal states.
learn.stat1: Developing countries received $45 billion in climate finance 2023
learn.stat2: IEA projects $1.2 trillion annual clean‑energy investment needed by 2030
learn.stat3: India’s renewable capacity grew to 210 GW in 2024
learn.stat4: Green Climate Fund approved 120 projects in Asia (2024)
